first_aid_treatment.specific_first_aid_treatment_provided_pr:
Immediately after the incident occurred at the community recreation center gymnasium, I (the reporting staff member) and another bystander initiated first aid while another staff member called 911. The injured party, Daniel Morales, slipped on a recently mopped section of the hallway floor outside the locker rooms and fell onto his right side, striking his right shoulder and the side of his head on the tile. 

Within approximately one minute of the fall, we helped him remain seated on the floor, encouraged him not to stand up too quickly, and assessed his level of consciousness and orientation by asking his name, the date, and where he was. He was alert and oriented but complained of dizziness, a headache, and sharp pain in his right shoulder. We visually assessed for obvious deformities, active bleeding, and indications of a more serious head injury. We noted mild swelling developing at the right temple area but no visible laceration or active bleeding.

We applied a cold compress (reusable ice pack wrapped in a clean towel) to the right side of his head to reduce swelling and discomfort. We also provided a second cold pack for his shoulder, again wrapped in a towel to avoid direct contact with the skin. We asked him to keep his right arm as still as possible and supported it in a comfortable position using a folded gym towel under his elbow.

His breathing was normal, skin color was appropriate, and he did not report chest pain or shortness of breath. We monitored his breathing and responsiveness continuously while waiting for EMS, checking in every couple of minutes by asking simple orientation questions and watching for any changes in his speech, pupil responsiveness, or ability to follow instructions.

We checked for any medical bracelets or necklaces and asked him directly about allergies, current medications, and any history of concussion or shoulder injuries. He reported no known drug allergies and no prior serious head injuries, but mentioned that he had mild shoulder tendinitis in the past. We did not administer any medications, including over‑the‑counter pain relievers, because we wanted EMS to evaluate him first and to avoid interfering with their assessment.

We cleared the surrounding area by asking other patrons to step back to give him space and privacy, and placed a folded sweatshirt behind his back to provide some support while he remained seated with his back gently resting against the wall. We advised him not to eat or drink anything until he was evaluated by EMS in case further treatment or imaging would be needed. We also documented the approximate time of the fall, the time 911 was called, and the sequence of first aid actions so that we could give a clear handoff to the paramedics when they arrived.
first_aid_treatment.medical_identification_tags: A stainless steel medical ID bracelet was observed on the injured party’s left wrist. The engraving read: "Type 2 Diabetes – Metformin – Emergency Contact: Laura M. (wife) (503) 555-9182." No additional medical ID necklaces or wallet cards were visibly present, although EMS was informed of the bracelet details upon their arrival.
first_aid_treatment.names_of_person_who_provided_first_aid: Primary: Olivia Chen (Front Desk Supervisor, CPR/First Aid certified). Assistance: Marcus Hill (Lifeguard, CPR/First Aid certified). Additional support and crowd control: Jenna Patel (Youth Programs Coordinator).
first_aid_treatment.treatment_provided:
Upon reaching the injured party within about one minute of the fall, Olivia Chen introduced herself, confirmed that Daniel could hear and respond, and performed an initial primary assessment: checking airway (clear, he was speaking), breathing (even and unlabored), and circulation (skin tone and temperature normal, no obvious major bleeding). Once it was confirmed that there was no immediate life‑threatening emergency such as cardiac arrest or severe bleeding, we moved into a focused secondary assessment.

Olivia asked Daniel to describe what happened in his own words. He explained that he had just left the locker room, didn’t see the wet floor sign, and his right foot slid out from under him, causing him to fall onto his right side. He denied losing consciousness and stated he "saw stars" for a moment but did not black out. He reported pain at a 6 out of 10 in the right shoulder and a 4 out of 10 headache on the right side of his head.

Olivia palpated gently around the right shoulder area, checking for obvious deformity, crepitus, or intense point tenderness that might suggest a fracture or dislocation. Daniel winced with movement but there was no visible deformity and he retained limited range of motion, though we advised him not to continue moving it. We opted for supportive care only and avoided attempting any repositioning.

Marcus retrieved two instant cold packs and clean towels from the first aid kit. A cold pack was wrapped in a towel and applied lightly to the area of impact on Daniel’s right temple, with frequent checks to ensure he was comfortable and that his skin was not becoming overly cold or irritated. A second cold pack, also wrapped, was placed gently over the outer aspect of his right shoulder to help reduce pain and swelling.

Throughout this time, Olivia monitored Daniel’s mental status by asking him to confirm his full name, date of birth, the current date, and his location every few minutes. His responses remained appropriate and consistent. She checked both pupils with a small penlight to ensure they were equal and reactive to light. She noted mild redness around the right temple but no obvious laceration or hematoma larger than a small raised area.

Daniel was encouraged to remain seated with his back supported by the wall and a folded sweatshirt behind him. We discouraged him from attempting to stand or walk due to the reported dizziness. We asked if he had taken any blood thinners or aspirin that day, and he stated that he only takes metformin for diabetes and a daily multivitamin.

We did not provide any food, drink, or medications, following our policy to avoid oral intake prior to EMS assessment after a head impact. We also ensured the floor area around him remained dry and that the wet floor sign was clearly visible to others to prevent additional accidents. Jenna managed bystanders, provided reassurance to Daniel’s 10‑year‑old son (who was present nearby), and kept the path clear for the arriving ambulance crew.

When EMS arrived, Olivia gave a verbal report including: time of fall, time 911 was called, initial observations, Daniel’s self‑reported symptoms, vital signs as observed (normal respiratory rate, normal skin color), lack of loss of consciousness, presence of mild dizziness and headache, and the first aid measures applied. All cold packs and towels were removed or repositioned as requested by EMS so they could conduct their own assessment.

No CPR, rescue breathing, Heimlich maneuver, or advanced airway management was required at any point. No AED was used, as Daniel did not exhibit any signs of cardiac arrest or life‑threatening arrhythmia.

injured_party_action_taken.taken_by_name_of_person: Paramedic James O’Neil and EMT Sarah Rodriguez, Portland Fire & Rescue Ambulance Unit 14
injured_party_action_taken.taken_to_identify_location: Legacy Good Samaritan Medical Center, 1015 NW 22nd Ave, Portland, OR 97210
injured_party_action_taken.transported_how: Transported by ambulance on stretcher with cervical precautions as a precautionary measure due to head impact and shoulder pain.
injured_party_action_taken.report_submitted_to: Center Director, Maple Grove Community Recreation Center (Attn: Director Karen Livingston)
